name: Kamadhenu Cow
description: Kamadhenu, also known as Surabhi, is a divine bovine goddess described in Hindu mythology as the mother of all cows and the source of all prosperity. She is a miraculous 'cow of plenty' who provides her owner whatever he desires and is often depicted as a white cow with a human head, peacock tail, and wings. Kamadhenu emerged from the churning of the cosmic ocean (Samudra Manthana) and was presented to the Saptarishis (seven great sages). She is considered sacred and a symbol of motherhood, abundance, and selfless giving. The possession of Kamadhenu is believed to bring good fortune and fulfill all wishes. Different accounts attribute her ownership to various deities and sages, including Indra, the king of gods, and Sage Vasishta. The cow's ability to grant wishes makes her a highly coveted treasure, often leading to conflicts between gods and demons. Kamadhenu's significance extends beyond her material gifts; she represents the nurturing aspect of the divine feminine and the importance of respecting all life forms. Her image is frequently used in Hindu art and iconography, symbolizing prosperity and the sanctity of cows. The legend of Kamadhenu serves as a reminder of the interconnectedness of all beings and the power of selfless giving.
subtitle: The Divine Cow of Plenty
origin: Churning of the Cosmic Ocean (Samudra Manthana)
currentLocation: Mythological realms; spiritually present in cows

keyMyths.title: Emergence from Samudra Manthana
keyMyths.description: Kamadhenu arose from the churning of the cosmic ocean, a pivotal event in Hindu cosmology. She was subsequently given to the Saptarishis, the seven great sages, signifying her divine status.

keyMyths.title: Conflict over Kamadhenu
keyMyths.description: Kings and deities have often fought over the possession of Kamadhenu due to her wish-granting abilities. This highlights the allure of material desires and the importance of spiritual detachment.

sources.author: Swami Venkatesananda
sources.description: Simplified retelling of the Ramayana, often mentioning Kamadhenu indirectly.
sources.text: Simplified retelling of the Ramayana, often mentioning Kamadhenu indirectly.
